coredata.dc:title: Impacts of graphene quantum dots on the optical, electrical and thermal properties of the archetypal conducting polymer PEDOT:PSS
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kong, Minghua
Garriga, Miquel
Reparaz, Juan Sebastián
Alonso, Maria Isabel
coredata.dc:description: We investigated the optical properties and thermal conductivity of blade-coated graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/PEDOT:PSS hybrid thin films by varying the content of GQDs. The optical properties were determined by spectroscopic ellipsometry in the range of 1.2–5.5 eV. Two dispersion models were used to analyze the optical properties of the films: the Bruggeman effective medium approximation (BEMA) for the hybrid films, and the Drude model combined with a Lorentzian oscillator for both the pure and the hybrid films, which provides insight into their electrical properties. As a novel finding, we observed that the optical anisotropy of PEDOT:PSS (Aldrich 483095) films is reduced after incorporating GQDs. Moreover, dedoping of the PEDOT chains is demonstrated upon increasing the content of GQDs within the hybrid films. Furthermore, the thermal conductivity shows a two-fold decrease as the GQDs fraction increases from 0 to 10 wt%. This result is understood considering that the GQDs act as local scattering centers, resulting in a decrease of the thermal conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
PEDOT:PSS
Optical anisotropy
Thermal conductivity
Bruggeman effective medium approximation
Drude model

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge funding from the Spanish Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovaci\u00F3n (MICINN) through grants PDC2021-121475-I00 and PID2020-119777GB-I00 and the Spanish Severo Ochoa Centre of Excellence program CEX2019-000917-S and CEX2023-001263-S, as well as AGAUR, Generalitat de Catalunya, Spain, grant 2021-SGR-00444. M.K. is grateful to the UAB PhD program in Materials Science in which she was enrolled and the China Scholarship Council for funding (CSC 201809370071). M.K. is sincerely thankful to Bernhard D\u00F6rling and Denise Estrada-Wiese for their corrections to the English and helpful comments. We thank Agust\u00EDn Mihi and Mariano Campoy-Quiles for their constant support and ideas. We acknowledge the assistance of the ICMAB-CSIC Scientific & Technical Services: Nanoquim Platform, Scanning Probe Microscopy, Electron Microscopy, and X-ray Diffraction.

The authors acknowledge funding from the Spanish Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovaci\u00F3n (MICINN) through grants PDC2021-121475-I00 and PID2020-119777GB-I00 and the Spanish Severo Ochoa Centre of Excellence program CEX2019-000917-S , as well as AGAUR, Generalitat de Catalunya , grant 2021-SGR-00444 . M.K. is grateful to the UAB PhD program in Materials Science in which she was enrolled and the China Scholarship Council for funding ( CSC 201809370071 ). M.K. is sincerely thankful to Bernhard D\u00F6rling and Denise Estrada-Wiese for their corrections to the English and helpful comments. We thank Agust\u00EDn Mihi and Mariano Campoy-Quiles for their constant support and ideas. We acknowledge the assistance of the ICMAB-CSIC Scientific & Technical Services: Nanoquim Platform, Scanning Probe Microscopy, Electron Microscopy, and X-ray Diffraction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Complex refractive index of GQDs/AI 4083 films with different mixing ratios.
Complex refractive index of GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films with different mixing ratios. Darker and lighter colored lines correspond to the extraordinary and ordinary refractive index respectively.

Out-of-plane thermal conductivity of blade-coated GQDs/PEDOT:PSS hybrid thin films measured by FDTR as a function of GQDs fraction. (a) GQDs/AI 4083; (b) GQDs/Aldrich 483095. Dashed lines are a guide to the eye.

Raman spectra of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films versus GQDs fraction. (a) GQDs/AI 4083; (b) GQDs/Aldrich 483095. Excitation wavelength is 785 nm. The different colors of Raman spectra represent different GQDs fractions, and the peak positions are annotated in the graphs. The Raman spectra were offset vertically to improve visibility.
Main peak linewidth versus GQDs fraction. HWHM represents the half width at half maximum of main peak.

Ellipsometry results of GQDs/AI 4083 films based on BEMA model. f and d are the fraction and thickness respectively, while the subscripts tag, fit and prof represent the target value during manufacturing, the fitted value from ellipsometry, and the measured value from profilometry respectively.

Ellipsometry results of GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films based on BEMA model.
Δ n
is the difference between
n e
and
n o
, indicating the birefringence.
Ellipsometry results of GQDs/AI 4083 films based on Drude-Lorentz model. ¨A¨ represents the amplitude of the generalized Lorentzian.

head.ce:title: Impacts of graphene quantum dots on the optical, electrical and thermal properties of the archetypal conducting polymer PEDOT:PSS

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institut de Ciència de Materials de Barcelona, ICMAB-CSIC, Campus UAB, Bellaterra 08193, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institut de Ciència de Materials de Barcelona

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institut de Ciència de Materials de Barcelona, ICMAB-CSIC, Campus UAB, Bellaterra 08193, Spain

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Physics, Southern University of Science and Technology, Shenzhen 518055, China (M. Kong).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southern University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We investigated the optical properties and thermal conductivity of blade-coated graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/PEDOT:PSS hybrid thin films by varying the content of GQDs. The optical properties were determined by spectroscopic ellipsometry in the range of 1.2–5.5 eV. Two dispersion models were used to analyze the optical properties of the films: the Bruggeman effective medium approximation (BEMA) for the hybrid films, and the Drude model combined with a Lorentzian oscillator for both the pure and the hybrid films, which provides insight into their electrical properties. As a novel finding, we observed that the optical anisotropy of PEDOT:PSS (Aldrich 483095) films is reduced after incorporating GQDs. Moreover, dedoping of the PEDOT chains is demonstrated upon increasing the content of GQDs within the hybrid films. Furthermore, the thermal conductivity shows a two-fold decrease as the GQDs fraction increases from 0 to 10 wt%. This result is understood considering that the GQDs act as local scattering centers, resulting in a decrease of the thermal conductivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene): poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS), one of the most extensively studied conducting polymers, is well known for its low thermal conductivity, low sheet resistance compared to other polymers, good transparency in the visible and good solution processability (Liang et al., 2019; Fan and Ouyang, 2019). Based on these properties, PEDOT:PSS is widely used in variable energy conversion and storage devices (Sun et al., 2015), e.g. flexible thermoelectric devices, fuel cells, etc. Most commonly, PEDOT:PSS acts as the electrode layer of solar cells. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a few nanometers in size, have caught much attention in recent years due to their unique properties, such as high electron mobility and high electrical conductivity, low toxicity, good solubility in various solvents, and excellent optical properties. It has been demonstrated that GQDs can be used in diverse areas related to energy harvesting and efficiency such as in photovoltaic devices and organic light-emitting diodes (Bacon et al., 2014).

Most studies of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films were made with photovoltaic applications in mind. The power conversion efficiency of solar cells is boosted dramatically after incorporating GQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer (Li et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2015). However, no investigations of optical constants and anisotropy were conducted. Yet, knowing the optical properties of the GQDs/PEDOT:PSS layer is necessary for different optical applications, for instance, for a proper modelling of the optical performance of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS layer in solar cells. Nowadays, it has been reported that PEDOT:PSS thin films are optically anisotropic displaying uniaxial behavior with the optic axis perpendicular to the film plane (Pettersson et al., 2002). This optical anisotropy is originated in the film morphology and is reflected in the anisotropic complex refractive indices of the films, which are usually determined by spectroscopic ellipsometry. However, reports about the optical anisotropy of PEDOT:PSS films are rare. Baytron P (1:1.25 wt ratio of PEDOT to PSS), PH500 and PH1000 (both 1:2.5), three formulations of PEDOT:PSS aqueous solution were reported to show optical anisotropy (Pettersson et al., 2002; Isoniemi et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2013). Our previous research indicated that low PSS content Aldrich 483095 (1:1.6) films show more pronounced optical anisotropy than high PSS content AI 4083 (1:6) films (Kong et al., 2022). On one hand, this result is tightly related to the PSS content because PSS films are optically isotropic. On the other hand, the optical anisotropy is most evident in the spectral region around the plasma energy which lies in the visible range for Aldrich 483095 films and in the near infrared range for AI 4083 films. The optical anisotropy of AI 4083 films is minimal in the visible-UV spectral range in which our samples were studied. Accordingly, when AI 4083 was mixed with optically isotropic GQDs, we used well-known isotropic models to fit the optical behavior of GQDs/AI 4083 films. In contrast, we used anisotropic models for GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films.

Previous works have also shown that GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films possess a great potential for thermoelectric applications (Kim, 2017; Du et al., 2018; Kepić et al., 2014). It is reported that the electrical conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films goes up with an increase in GQDs content (Du et al., 2018; Kepić et al., 2014), or rises at first and then goes down (Kim, 2017; Wang et al., 2021). One goal of the current article is to ascertain the electrical properties of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films by connecting them with the optical information. Notably, when investigating GQDs/PEDOT:PSS hybrid films, considerably less effort has been made on determining their thermal conductivity due to the difficulty in testing. Wang et al. (2021) used the laser flash method to measure the thermal conductivity and found that the thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS (formulation PH1000) films with DMSO treatment slightly increases from 0.8 W·m−1·K−1 to 0.95 W·m−1·K−1 at 363 K when the mass ratio of GQDs to PEDOT:PSS increases from 0.1 % to 10 %. However, the underlying reasons for the thermal conductivity change were not discussed. Further investigation on the thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films is clearly needed.

In this paper, we report the influence of GQDs content on the optical, electrical properties and thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS hybrid thin films. We investigated two different formulations of PEDOT:PSS solutions (Aldrich 483095 and AI 4083). We used two different approaches to model the optical properties in order to deduce different parameters of interest. We used the Bruggeman effective medium approximation (BEMA) model to fit the ellipsometric data of the hybrids by using the optical constants obtained from the pure reference samples. We compared the fitted thicknesses to those determined by profilometry. We also compared the calculated film composition based on the BEMA model with the target value in the fabrication process. Since Aldrich 483095 films show significant optical anisotropy, we paid close attention to the change of anisotropy after incorporating GQDs. In the other approach, we analyzed the ellipsometric data within the framework of the Drude approximation, by treating each hybrid sample as a new, homogeneous material. The fitted parameters obtained from the Drude model provide further insight into the influence of the GQDs content on the electrical properties. Furthermore, we also addressed the influence of the GQDs content on the thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films. In order to understand the thermal conductivity drop, we carried out a structural and morphological characterization of the GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films.

We used commercial glass microscope slides as substrates. Prior to film deposition, the glass substrates were ultrasonically cleaned for 15 min in soap, acetone, and isopropanol (IPA), successively. After that, the substrates were transferred into an UV ozone cleaner device for longer than 30 min to improve their wettability. GQDs (Sigma-Aldrich 900726) were dispersed in Milli-Q water by sonication. A bulk GQDs film was fabricated by drop casting 45 µL of the GQDs aqueous solution onto a pre-cleaned glass substrate (1.25 cm × 1.25 cm). The as-cast film was then placed on a hot plate to drive off the excess solvent, with the hotplate temperature incrementally increased from 40 to 60 °C in 5 °C intervals (5 min each). The final thermal step at 60 °C lasted 10 min, after which a dried GQDs film was obtained.

The PEDOT:PSS aqueous solution was filtered with a syringe filter (PTFE membrane, 0.22 μm pore-size) to remove the larger particles. A filtered PEDOT:PSS solution from Sigma-Aldrich 483095 with a dispersion content of 1.3 % in water was used without mixing with isopropanol (IPA). On the other hand, the filtered PEDOT:PSS solution from Heraeus AI 4083 with a dispersion content of 1.3–1.7 % in water was mixed with IPA at a volume ratio of 1:2. The IPA-mixed PEDOT:PSS aqueous solution was stirred for 1 h. Filtered PEDOT:PSS solutions with or without IPA addition were mixed with GQDs aqueous solution by stirring. Subsequently, the well-mixed GQDs/PEDOT:PSS solution was blade-coated on a glass substrate. In particular, the AI 4083 film and GQDs/AI 4083 films were deposited at a blade speed of 70–90 mm/s at 65 °C, while the Aldrich 483095 film and GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films were prepared at a blade speed of 40–60 mm/s at 110 °C. All samples were always kept inside a moisture and oxygen free atmosphere after fabrication. The mass ratios of GQDs to PEDOT:PSS we mainly investigated are 0, 1/10, 1/2 and 1/1. We used the mass ratio to name the samples.

The thermal conductivity of thin films was determined using a custom-built frequency-domain thermoreflectance (FDTR) setup (Regner et al., 2013). The film thickness is an essential input parameter for this FDTR technique. Error bars of FDTR measurements were calculated by considering the sensitivities of the parameters in the thermal model. A variable angle spectroscopic ellipsometer (SEMILAB GES5E) was used to determine the film thickness, and optical properties in the range of 1.2–5.4 eV. The film thicknesses were also measured with a profilometer (KLA Tencor D-500) for comparison with the ones obtained from the ellipsometric measurements. A custom setup consisting of a sourcemeter (Keithley 2604B) and a testing stage with micromanipulators to contact the sample was used to determine the sheet resistance of thin films. And then the electrical conductivity is calculated based on the formula:
σ = 1 / ρ = L / R w d
, where
σ
is electrical conductivity, ρ is electrical resistivity,
R
is the measured resistance,
d
is the film thickness,
L
is the length, and
w
is the width. The Raman spectra were measured with a Raman spectrometer (WITec alpha300 RA) at an excitation wavelength of 785 nm, using a 100x objective and 2 mW laser power. The surface topography of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films was characterized by atomic force microscopy (Keysight 5100). Scanning electron microscopy images were acquired in a QUANTA FEI 200 FEG-ESEM and x-ray diffraction in a Bruker D8 Discover diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation.

All films looked transparent to the naked eye and no macroscopic differences between them could be appreciated. Optical micrographs showed some more detail (see Supplementary Fig. S1) but no clear systematic differences were evident. We attempted to characterize further the composed nanostructure of some films using Scanning Electron microscopy. Images are presented in Supplementary Fig. S2. Surface topography in a finer scale was observed by atomic force microscopy, reported in Supplementary Figs. S3 and S4.

The Bruggeman effective medium approximation (BEMA) model was used for fitting the ellipsometric measurements of the hybrids, utilizing the known optical constants of the pure films obtained in earlier experiments. Specifically, we fitted the relative volume ratio of the components and the film thickness. The anisotropic model in which the optic axis is perpendicular to the film was used to fit GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films, while the isotropic model is suitable to fit GQDs/AI 4083 films. The isotropic BEMA model is expressed as follows (Tompkins and Hilfiker, 2016):
(1)
f A
ε ∼
A
-
ε ∼
EMA
ε ∼
A
+ 2
ε ∼
EMA
+
f B
ε ∼
B
-
ε ∼
EMA
ε ∼
B
+ 2
ε ∼
EMA
= 0
where
ε ∼
EMA
is the effective complex dielectric function of the composite material,
ε ∼
A
and
ε ∼
B
are the complex dielectric functions of components A and B,
f A
and
f B
are the volume fractions of A and B and satisfy the relation:
f A
+
f B
= 1
. To deal with an effective medium composed by the mixture of a uniaxial PEDOT:PSS and an isotropic GQDs phase, a tensorial model should be used and solved numerically, as explained in the Supplementary information. However, in this particular case, the range of variability of the three different dielectric function values is rather limited and it is sufficient to analyze the measured effective data
ε ∼
EMA
o
and
ε ∼
EMA
e
considering that each one of these components obey Eq. (1). That is, using
ε ∼
A
o
and
ε ∼
B
for the ordinary component and
ε ∼
A
e
and
ε ∼
B
for the extraordinary one, where A is the uniaxial material and B the isotropic phase.

The optical constants of pure PEDOT:PSS and GQDs reference films are shown in Figs. 1 and 2
. Although GQDs/PEDOT:PSS is actually a three-component system, we assumed it as a two-component system in which PEDOT:PSS is regarded as a single, new material. Note that PEDOT:PSS cannot be modelled using BEMA because its components interact. Moreover, when evaluating Aldrich 483095 films, we considered the isotropic GQDs phase for the ordinary and extraordinary components of PEDOT:PSS with a common volume fraction of GQDs during the regression analysis. To summarize the fitted anisotropy, we indicate the birefringence
Δ n =
n e
-
n o
(
n e
: extraordinary part of refractive index, describing out-of-plane direction;
n o
: ordinary part of refractive index, describing in-plane direction.) at some arbitrary energy which seems representative, in this case 2 eV.

Tables 1 and 2
show the best fit results of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films based on the BEMA model. The fitted GQDs fractions are in good agreement with the target values in the fabrication process, which means GQDs are well dispersed in the hybrid films. It is worth noting that the target value in the fabrication process is a weight fraction, whereas the fitted value is a volume fraction. The good consistency between these two fractions indicates that the density of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films is close to 1 g/cm3, which makes sense based on the facts that the density of PEDOT:PSS film is 1.2 g/cm3 (Liu et al., 2015) and the density of bulk graphene quantum dots provided by the manufacturer is 0.25–0.55 g/cm3. However, the largest difference between the fitted value and the target value lies in the 1/1 hybrid films. One possible reason is that GQDs do not disperse very well in 1/1 films. In this case, the maximum GQDs fraction should be chosen between 33 % and 50 % in order to obtain homogeneous composite films. Another possibility is that the change in the film density results in the fraction difference in 1/1 films. When the GQDs fraction reaches 50 %, the density of 1/1 hybrid films is not 1 g/cm3 anymore, hence, there is a discrepancy between the weight fraction and the volume fraction. In theory, if the density of 1/1 film is less than 1 g/cm3, then the volume fraction of GQDs should be larger than their weight fraction. Nevertheless, this is not the case in the 1/1 GQDs/AI 4083 film. Accordingly, we think the main reason for the discrepancy between the fitted and the target values is the bad dispersibility of GQDs at higher concentration in 1/1 hybrid films. Moreover, as illustrated in Tables 1 and 2, the fitted film thicknesses are consistent with the ones detected by profilometry. The large thickness difference in the 1/1 GQDs/AI 4083 film probably results from the fact that ellipsometry and profilometry did not probe the exact same point. Even though we tried to do both measurements near the same position, this was approximate. More importantly, the birefringence
Δ n
goes down with the increase of the GQDs fraction in Table 2. This reveals that the optical anisotropy of PEDOT:PSS (Aldrich 483095) films is reduced by mixing with GQDs. When the GQDs fraction is 50 %, the GQDs/Aldrich 483095 composite film becomes optically isotropic.

The full results of the complex refractive index for GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films of both formulations are shown in Figs. 1 and 2. Fig. 1 shows the complex refractive index of GQDs/AI 4083 films with different mixing ratios. As the GQDs fraction increases, the n, k curves of hybrid films gradually shift away from those of pure PEDOT:PSS and approach those of pure GQDs. The n, k curves of the 1/10 film almost overlap with those of the pure PEDOT:PSS film. Additionally, the very low k values in the 2.0–5.0 eV region indicate the good optical transparency of PEDOT:PSS and GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films in the visible spectral region and part of the UV region. Fig. 2 shows the same information for mixtures of GQDs with another formulation of PEDOT:PSS, namely Aldrich 483095, where we observe significant anisotropic behavior. It is clear from Fig. 2 that ne is always larger than no . However, the difference is higher for higher PEDOT:PSS content. In the 1/1 film, ne is almost equal to no indicating that the 1/1 film becomes optically isotropic. On the contrary, ke is smaller than ko (for photon energy below 5.2 eV). The shape of ke curves of hybrid films is more similar to that of GQDs in the range of 1.2–4.5 eV.

Additionally, we fitted the measured ellipsometric angles
tan ψ
and
cos Δ
of GQDs/AI 4083 films using an isotropic Drude model, effectively treating each film as a single-phase, optically homogeneous material. In particular, the Drude model was used to fit the film thickness and the usual Drude parameters which reflect the electrical properties of GQDs/AI 4083 films. The Drude model is suitable to describe the metallic-like behavior given by the bipolaron band. Generalized Lorentzians take into account other observed contributions, such as the polaron band or interband transitions (Kong et al., 2022; Laskarakis et al., 2017; Laskarakis et al., 2013; Losurdo et al., 2002). We used a combination of the Drude-Lorentz model to determine the electrical properties of GQDs/AI 4083 films. The final model used is:
(2)
ε
E
=
ε ∞
-
E p
2
E
2
+ i E
Γ p
+
∑ j
A j
e
i
φ j
E -
E j
+ i
Γ j
where
E p
is the plasma energy,
Γ p
is the Drude broadening,
ε ∞
is the high frequency dielectric constant.
A j
is the amplitude,
φ j
is the phase,
E j
is the transition energy and
Γ j
is the broadening of Lorentz peak j. The fitted parameters from the Drude model can be used to estimate the carrier concentration
N P
and mobility μ which are given as follows:
(3)
N P
c
m
- 3
=
7.2525 × 10
20
E
p
2
ε ∞
m
∗
(4)
μ
cm
2
/ V s
=
7.2739
Γ p
m
∗
where
m
∗
is the effective mass of the charge carriers and both
Γ p
and
E p
are expressed in eV. The electrical resistivity is related to the obtained Drude parameters, and it is calculated according to:
(5)
ρ
Ω · c m
=
4 π h
ε
0
Γ
p
ε
∞
E
p
2
= 0.58695
Γ
p
ε
∞
E
p
2
where
E p
and
Γ p
are expressed in eV.

Here, we do not show the fitted thicknesses any more since they are almost the same as the ones from the BEMA model. Fig. 3 shows the Drude parameters and conductivity of GQDs/AI 4083 films versus GQDs fraction. The plasma energy decreases linearly with GQDs fraction, suggesting the decreasing bipolaron concentration in the composite films. On the other hand, the high frequency dielectric constant rises linearly, which means the electronic density of composite films increases with GQDs fraction. Moreover, the Drude broadening changes slightly with GQDs fraction, fluctuating between 0.65 eV and 0.85 eV. This implies that the carrier mobility remains rather stable with GQDs fraction. However, there is a downward trend in the calculated conductivity. It can be concluded that the main factor that controls the conductivity of GQDs/AI 4083 films is the plasma energy, i.e. the bipolaron concentration. We note that there is also a gradual decline in the measured dc electrical conductivity of GQDs/AI 4083 films in Supplementary Fig. S6a, suggesting the calculated values can give a good prediction of the experimental trends. However, the measured conductivity is much smaller than the calculated one. This discrepancy can be attributed to the fact that the presence of macroscopic defects affects the electrical measurement (Humlíček et al., 1998). In addition, the model used to estimate the conductivity is simplistic, also leading to this difference. Note that previous research showed that the electrical conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films increased with increasing GQDs content (Du et al., 2018; Kepić et al., 2014), or increased at first and then decreased (Kim, 2017; Wang et al., 2021), whereas we mainly observe a decrease. In other works electrical conductivity of PEDOT:PSS films is enhanced after doping, such as Ca and Mg doping (Yağci and Özdemir, 2022), ZnI2 doping (Krobthong et al., 2023), H2SO4 doping (Zhang et al., 2020), etc. The different electrical behaviors may result from different PEDOT:PSS formulations, deposition or treatment methods, as well as percolation among conducting particles or domains. The difference in fabrication process leads to different PEDOT doping level, PSS amount, film morphology or crystalline orientation, affecting the charge transport in different ways.

Table 3 shows the fitting results of GQDs/AI 4083 films based on the Drude-Lorentz model. ¨A¨ represents the amplitude of the generalized Lorentzian, which gives information about the polaron concentration according to the conventional density functional theory (DFT) (Zozoulenko et al., 2019). It is clear from Table 3 that polarons appear in the hybrid films but are not detectable in the AI 4083 film, which is in accordance with the widely accepted idea that the charge carriers in PEDOT:PSS films are mainly bipolarons. Here, it should be pointed out that the charge carriers in PEDOT backbones are both positively charged polarons and bipolarons. Generally, the charge carriers in lightly and moderately doped conducting polymers are polarons, and bipolarons become the dominant charge carriers in the heavily doped state (Brédas et al., 1987). Since PEDOT:PSS is heavily doped, the charge carriers in pristine PEDOT:PSS are most likely bipolarons (Gangopadhyay et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2020). In sum, the appearance of polarons and the decreasing bipolaron concentration reveal the dedoping of PEDOT chains from bipolaron to polaron as the GQDs fraction increases.

Fig. 4 illustrates the dependence of the out-of-plane thermal conductivity on GQDs fraction in blade-coated GQDs/PEDOT:PSS hybrid thin films. The thermal conductivity decreases rapidly when adding 0–10 wt% GQDs in the PEDOT:PSS solution and reaches a plateau with additional GQDs. GQDs/AI 4083 films and GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films display a similar changing trend and range (0.55–0.2 W·m−1·K−1). Here, we note that three more ratios (1/200, 1/100 and 1/20) are included in Fig. 4. However, since they are not significantly different from pure PEDOT:PSS, we focused on the 1/10, 1/2 and 1/1 ratios.

In order to find out the reason for the thermal conductivity drop, we first verified the effect of charge carriers on the thermal conductivity. The electrical conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films was measured and the results are shown in Supplementary Fig. S6. It is widely accepted that the thermal conductivity has contributions from the electronic part (charge carriers) and the lattice part (phonons) (Pereira Gonalves et al., 2010). The electronic part of the thermal conductivity (κe ) is proportional to the electrical conductivity (σ) and can be computed through the Wiedemann-Franz law (Weathers et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2022): κe = LσT. Using the Sommerfeld value 2.45 × 10−8 V2 K−2 for the Lorenz constant L of PEDOT:PSS (Liu et al., 2015), we calculated the electronic thermal conductivity using the maximum value of the electrical conductivity. The resulting κe is 1.5 × 10−5 W·m−1·K−1 in GQDs/AI 4083 films and 1.8 × 10−5 W·m−1·K−1 in GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films. Since the calculated electronic thermal conductivity is much smaller than the measured total one, we conclude that the thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films is dominated by the phonon contribution.

To further understand the thermal conductivity behavior, Raman spectroscopy was used to investigate structural variations. All studied films were amorphous as found by x-ray diffraction (Supplementary Fig. S7) but Raman spectroscopy could detect changes in the short range order structure of the films. Fig. 5 shows the Raman spectra of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films versus GQDs fraction at 785 nm excitation wavelength. Raman scattering is mainly sensitive to PEDOT vibrations, whereas PSS and GQDs vibrations are not detectable in our experimental conditions. As the GQDs fraction increases, red shifts occur in the symmetric Cα = Cβ stretching vibration, from 1432 cm−1 to 1417 cm−1 for GQDs/AI 4083 films and from 1424 cm−1 to 1410 cm−1 for GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films. Generally, a red shift indicates an increased average conjugation length (Zheng et al., 1993; Tsoi et al., 2011). This is possibly due to the dedoping of PEDOT chains from bipolaron to polaron or neutral state (Zhu et al., 2015; Lee et al., 2014) although Raman alone does not provide an unambiguous proof. In our case, this dedoping has been confirmed by the ellipsometry results in Section 3.2. The red shift indicates that PEDOT:PSS polymer chains tend to be more ordered with the increase of GQDs fraction (Bragg et al., 2016; Veerender et al., 2014). In addition, we observed a change in the main peak linewidth with GQDs fraction, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . The most intense band becomes narrower as the GQDs fraction increases. The linewidth of this intense band arises from the distribution of conjugation lengths (Kong et al., 2022; Sauvajol et al., 1990). A narrower band implies a more homogeneous distribution of conjugated domains. Our previous research result suggests that improved distribution homogeneity and more ordered polymer chains would contribute to increase the thermal conductivity of pure polymer (Kong, 2022); instead of decreasing it. Therefore, the observed local structural variations cannot be related to the drop of thermal conductivity observed here.

Moreover, to verify whether the aforementioned thermal conductivity results could be correlated to film morphological changes, we carried out an AFM characterization of the GQDs/PEDOT:PSS composite films. The topography images of GQDs/AI 4083 films and GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films were shown in Supplementary Figs. S3 and S4. We did not observe a clear morphology difference in GQDs/AI 4083 films with different GQDs fractions. Note that the thermal conductivity decreases significantly from the PEDOT:PSS film to the 1/10 hybrid film. However, the roughness and the apparent phase separation of these two films hardly change. Similar results are also obtained for GQDs/Aldrich 483095 films. In conclusion, changes in morphology can neither explain the observed drop in thermal conductivity.

Therefore, we presume that the most plausible mechanism which controls the thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films is that GQDs act as scattering centers. As the GQDs fraction increases, the phonon scattering becomes stronger, thus resulting in the decrease of thermal conductivity (Kim et al., 2012; Song et al., 2013; Lim et al., 2019; Agnihotri et al., 2015). Wang et al. (2021) observed a slight increase in thermal conductivity of GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films with increasing GQDs content, which is different from our result. The possible reasons for this discrepancy are diverse. Firstly, their films were fabricated by vacuum filtration, whereas our fabrication method is blade coating. The fabrication method has a large influence on the thermoelectric performance of PEDOT:PSS films by affecting the molecular packing and PEDOT backbone orientation (Deng and Chen, 2021). Secondly, their films were specifically treated with DMSO, leading to the associated structural and morphological changes (Cassinelli et al., 2021; Ouyang et al., 2015; Huang et al., 2021), thus influencing the charge transport. Moreover, their GQDs size is around 10 nm, whereas our GQDs size is less than 5 nm. When adding the same amount of GQDs, the smaller the GQDs size is, the stronger the phonon scattering is expected to be.

In this work we have investigated the optical properties of PEDOT:PSS films blended with GQDs by spectroscopic ellipsometry and Raman scattering. The observed variations with GQDs content provide insight into both structural changes and evolution of the electrical properties. The calculated GQDs fractions based on the BEMA model are in good agreement with the target values in the fabrication process. As a remarkable finding, we observed that there is a gradual decline in the optical anisotropy of PEDOT:PSS (Aldrich 483095) films with increasing GQDs fraction. Furthermore, the decreasing bipolaron concentration and the appearance of polarons in the optical spectra prove the dedoping of PEDOT chains in GQDs/PEDOT:PSS films as the GQDs fraction increases.

The thermal conductivity of PEDOT:PSS films can be significantly tuned by mixing with GQDs, depending on the amount of GQDs. We have found a two-fold decrease for the thermal conductivity as the GQDs fraction increases from 0 to 10 wt%. The most plausible mechanism is that GQDs act as scattering centers and the phonon scattering plays a key role in the thermal conductivity change. Mastering how to tune the thermal conductivity and understanding the reason for thermal conductivity changes are important for optimizing thermoelectric conversion efficiency.

Minghua Kong: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Miquel Garriga: Visualization, Software, Formal analysis. Juan Sebastián Reparaz: Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Maria Isabel Alonso: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge funding from the Spanish Ministerio de Ciencia e Innovación (MICINN) through grants PDC2021-121475-I00 and PID2020-119777GB-I00 and the Spanish Severo Ochoa Centre of Excellence program CEX2019-000917-S and CEX2023-001263-S, as well as AGAUR, Generalitat de Catalunya, Spain, grant 2021-SGR-00444. M.K. is grateful to the UAB PhD program in Materials Science in which she was enrolled and the China Scholarship Council for funding (CSC 201809370071). M.K. is sincerely thankful to Bernhard Dörling and Denise Estrada-Wiese for their corrections to the English and helpful comments. We thank Agustín Mihi and Mariano Campoy-Quiles for their constant support and ideas. We acknowledge the assistance of the ICMAB-CSIC Scientific & Technical Services: Nanoquim Platform, Scanning Probe Microscopy, Electron Microscopy, and X-ray Diffraction.
